text: This patient presented to my clinic with a near obstructing mass of the sigmoid colon. Biopsy showed adenocarcinoma. CEA was measured at fourteen point two. CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis did not show any evidence of metastatic disease. During colonoscopy, a tattoo was placed adjacent to the mass.

Upon entering the abdomen, the surgeon encounters adhesions in the right lower quadrant due to the desmoplastic reaction of the colon cancer. The separation of these planes results in moderate bleeding. A peritoneal margin is excised and visually confirmed to be negative for local cancer spread.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection of the mesocolon, incising its base to separate it from the retroperitoneal space. A broad window is created in the mesocolon, with care taken to keep the ureter and gonadal vessels in the retroperitoneal space. Vermiculation of the ureter confirms its identity.

The surgeon identifies the left ureter medial to the gonadal vessels and uses a vessel sealer to dissect anteriorly and distally towards the rectosigmoid junction. The inferior mesenteric artery is transected at its base using a vascular stapler, while the superior hemorrhoidal arteries remain anterior to the dissection plane. The proximal transection point is identified, and the surgeon begins transecting the mesentery towards the colon.

The surgeon observes the proximal margin of perfusion in the redundant sigmoid colon after injecting ICG. The mesocolon has been completely transected, and the blood supply to the specimen is removed. The surgeon scores the colon with cautery at the proximal margin to mark the site for later stapling.

The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon from the pelvis, revealing the distal margin of perfusion. The distal margin of the colon cancer is visibly sufficient, and the surgeon scores the distal margin of perfusion using cautery to mark the site before the ICG diffuses from the tissue.

The surgeon prepares the specimen for distal and proximal transection, performing an intracorporeal anastomosis. A 29mm EEA anvil is introduced into the abdomen, with a proline suture tied to the anvil to create a purse string for suturing the anvil into the proximal colon. After transecting the specimen, the sigmoid colon is removed from the pelvis and set aside to facilitate the anastomosis.

The surgeon removes the proximal staple line using scissors and electric cautery. The EEA anvil is placed into the proximal colon, and the posterior enclosure is performed by holding the mucosal edges of the colon at the base of the anvil to ensure a solid anastomosis, using a 2-0 proline suture.

The distal portion of the EEA stapler is inserted through the rectum, and the two components of the stapler are joined to create the end-to-end anastomosis. A second dose of ICG is injected to confirm adequate perfusion of the anastomosis.

The surgeon robotically sutures the anterior anastomosis for reinforcement, followed by performing a standard leak test. The proximal colon is occluded, and air is insufflated distally, with no air bubbles observed, indicating a negative leak test.

This patient was discharged home on post-operative day two. The pathology report identified a nine point five centimeter colon cancer. All margins were negative. Twenty seven lymph nodes were collected, all of which were negative for metastatic disease.
